content:
How better debouncing and caching cut costs by 85% without changing the feature.

4 min read
Just now

--

You ship the feature. The metrics look green.
The standup is smooth. Nobody notices anything wrong — until the AWS bill lands and your engineering manager goes very quiet in Slack for about four minutes.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

That was us, eight months ago. One endpoint. One overlooked mistake. Four thousand dollars a month, quietly bleeding out.
The Endpoint That Looked Fine
The endpoint was a search feature for a lending dashboard. Every time a user typed into the search bar, the frontend fired a request. Keystroke by keystroke, in real time, to a backend that hit the database with a full-text query each time.
It worked perfectly in development. Seventeen users on staging felt snappy. In production, with 3,000 daily active users typing at different speeds across different network conditions, we were generating roughly 800,000 API calls per day from that single field.
The backend query itself was not even slow. That was the trap. Because it was fast, nobody flagged it. Response times looked normal in Datadog. The error rate was zero.
